_provenance: Claude-authored original Gaokao reading, batch D1. ~220-280 word passages, 4 MCQs each (detail/inference/main idea/attitude/vocab). Gaokao difficulty. DeepSeek not used.

sets.passage: Few people have heard of Mary Anderson, yet millions of drivers owe their safety to her. In 1902, while visiting New York City on a cold, wet day, Anderson noticed that the driver of her streetcar had to keep stopping to wipe snow from the windscreen by hand, opening the window and leaning out into the freezing air. Most passengers simply accepted this as a normal inconvenience. Anderson, however, began to wonder whether there might be a better way. Back home, she designed a device with a rubber blade that the driver could move across the glass using a handle inside the vehicle. In 1903, she received a patent for the first practical windscreen wiper. At the time, few took her seriously; one company even rejected her idea, claiming it had no commercial value. Anderson never made much money from her invention, and her patent expired before cars became common. Yet within twenty years, as the motor car spread across the world, windscreen wipers became standard equipment on every vehicle. Today we cannot imagine driving in rain or snow without them. Anderson's story is a quiet reminder that great ideas are not always recognised at first, and that the inventions we most take for granted often began with one person refusing to accept that things could not be improved.

questions.prompt: What problem did Anderson notice on the streetcar?
options: The driver had to keep stopping to wipe snow by hand.

questions.prompt: How did people react to her invention at first?
options: They did not take her seriously.
They paid her a lot of money.

questions.prompt: Why does the writer say the windscreen wiper is 'taken for granted'?
options: Because it is now so common that we rarely think about it.

questions.prompt: What is the writer's main point?
options: Great ideas are not always recognised at first.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 司机得手动停下刮雪)、细节(B 起初没人当真)、词义/推断(B 太常见以致被忽视)、主旨(B 伟大创意未必一开始被认可)。

sets.passage: In the heart of Milan, two tall apartment towers rise above the city, but they look like no ordinary buildings. Their balconies are covered with more than nine hundred trees and thousands of plants, so that from a distance the towers appear to be growing out of a forest. Known as the 'Vertical Forest', this remarkable project was designed to bring nature back into the crowded modern city. The benefits are more than visual. The plants help to clean the air by absorbing harmful gases and dust, and they release oxygen. In summer, the leaves provide shade and keep the apartments cool, reducing the need for air conditioning. The greenery also attracts birds and insects, creating a small island of wildlife high above the busy streets. Of course, such buildings are not simple to create. The trees must be carefully chosen to survive strong winds at great heights, and skilled gardeners are needed to care for them. Critics also point out that the towers are expensive, and that planting trees on balconies cannot, by itself, solve a city's environmental problems. Still, the Vertical Forest has inspired similar projects in other countries. Its real value, perhaps, lies less in the air it cleans than in the question it forces us to ask: in the cities of the future, must concrete and nature really remain enemies?

questions.prompt: What makes the Milan towers special?
options: They are the tallest in the world.
Their balconies are covered with trees and plants.
They are made entirely of glass.

questions.prompt: How do the plants help in summer?
options: They make a lot of noise.
They provide shade and keep the apartments cool.
They block out all the light.

questions.prompt: What do critics say about the towers?
options: They are expensive and cannot solve a city's problems alone.
The plants on them are fake.
Nobody wants to live in them.

questions.prompt: What question does the project finally raise?
options: Whether concrete and nature must remain enemies in future cities.
Whether Milan is the best city.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 阳台覆满树木花草)、细节(B 夏天遮荫降温、少用空调)、细节(B 造价高、单靠它解决不了城市环境问题)、主旨/推断(B 未来城市里混凝土与自然是否必为敌)。

sets.passage: When shared bikes first appeared in Chinese cities a few years ago, they were hailed as a perfect solution to urban transport. With a smartphone, anyone could unlock a bike, ride it anywhere, and leave it for the next user. The bikes were cheap, convenient, and good for the environment, reducing both traffic and pollution. Within months, millions of brightly coloured bikes filled the streets. However, the rapid success soon brought serious problems. Companies, eager to win customers, flooded the cities with far more bikes than were needed. Pavements became blocked with bikes left carelessly everywhere. Many were damaged or broken, and huge 'bike graveyards' appeared on the edges of cities, where thousands of unused bikes were piled up to rust. The lesson, experts say, is not that shared bikes are a bad idea, but that good ideas can fail when growth is too fast and poorly managed. The companies had focused on expanding as quickly as possible, without thinking carefully about the consequences. Today, the shared-bike industry is smaller but healthier. Fewer companies remain, rules limit the number of bikes, and cities work with companies to manage parking. Used wisely, shared bikes still offer real benefits. Their story is a useful warning that, in business as in life, faster is not always better.

questions.prompt: Why were shared bikes praised at first?
options: They were cheap, convenient and good for the environment.

questions.prompt: What problem soon appeared?
options: Too many bikes blocked pavements and piled up unused.
Nobody used the bikes at all.

questions.prompt: What is the main lesson, according to experts?
options: Shared bikes are a bad idea.
Good ideas can fail when growth is too fast and poorly managed.

questions.prompt: What is the shared-bike industry like today?

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 便宜便捷环保)、细节(B 车太多堵人行道、堆积生锈)、推断/主旨(B 增长过快且管理不善会使好点子失败)、细节(B 如今更小但更健康)。

sets.passage: Plastic has become one of the great problems of our age. It is cheap, strong and useful, but most of it lasts for hundreds of years, polluting our land and seas. Now, scientists are developing new materials that could offer a solution: plastics that break down naturally and harmlessly. One promising material is made not from oil, like ordinary plastic, but from plants such as corn and sugarcane. Products made from it can look and feel just like normal plastic, yet under the right conditions they will decompose within months instead of centuries. Some types break down in ordinary soil; others are even safe to eat. However, these new materials are not a perfect answer. Many of them only break down properly in special industrial facilities, which most cities do not yet have. If they are thrown into the sea or an ordinary rubbish bin, they may last almost as long as ordinary plastic. They are also more expensive to produce, which makes companies reluctant to use them. Most scientists agree that new materials alone will not save us. The deeper change must be in how we behave: using less plastic, reusing what we have, and recycling properly. New materials may help, but they are only part of a much larger solution that depends, in the end, on all of us.

questions.prompt: What are the new plastics made from?
options: Plants such as corn and sugarcane.

questions.prompt: What is a problem with the new materials?
options: Many only break down in special facilities that most cities lack.
They cannot be made at all.

questions.prompt: What do most scientists believe?
options: New materials alone will solve everything.
New materials alone are not enough; our behaviour must change.
Plastic should never be used again.

questions.prompt: What is the passage mainly about?
options: New biodegradable plastics and their limits.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 以玉米甘蔗等植物制成)、细节(B 多数需专门工业设施才降解)、推断(B 仅靠新材料不够、须改变行为)、主旨(B 可降解塑料及其局限)。

sets.passage: Have you ever sat down to read a book, only to find that after a few minutes your hand has reached for your phone? If so, you are not alone. Many people today complain that they can no longer concentrate as they once did, and a growing number of scientists believe that our digital devices may be partly to blame. The problem lies in the way many apps and websites are designed. To make money, they are built to capture and hold our attention for as long as possible. Bright notifications, endless feeds and small rewards keep us checking our screens again and again. Each interruption may seem harmless, but together they train our brains to expect constant stimulation, making the quiet, patient focus needed for deep reading or thinking harder to achieve. This matters more than it might appear. The ability to concentrate deeply is essential not only for study and work, but for understanding complex ideas and even for our own happiness. A mind that is always jumping from one thing to another rarely feels calm. The good news is that focus, like a muscle, can be strengthened. By setting aside time without screens, turning off notifications, and practising single tasks, we can slowly rebuild our power of attention. In a world designed to distract us, learning to focus has become a quiet act of self-control.

questions.prompt: What do many people complain about today?
options: That they can no longer concentrate as they once did.
That the internet is too slow.

questions.prompt: Why are many apps designed to hold our attention?
Why does deep focus matter, according to the writer?
options: It is essential for study, understanding ideas, and happiness.
It is only useful for scientists.

questions.prompt: What does the writer suggest about focus?
options: We should throw away all our phones.
Focus, like a muscle, can be strengthened with practice.
Distraction cannot be helped at all.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 抱怨注意力不如从前)、细节(B 为赚钱而抓住注意力)、推断(B 深度专注对学习/理解/幸福都重要)、主旨/态度(B 专注如肌肉可练就)。

sets.passage: At the age of ten, Liu Wei lost both of his arms in an accident. Doctors said he would never lead a normal life. Yet today, Liu is known across China not for what he lost, but for what he achieved: he is a pianist who plays beautifully with his feet. The road was far from easy. After the accident, Liu fell into despair, refusing to leave his home. But slowly, encouraged by his mother, he began to look for things he could still do. He had always loved music, so he decided to try to play the piano with his toes. People laughed at the idea. It seemed impossible: the piano was made for hands, not feet, and every teacher he asked refused to take him. So Liu taught himself. For years, he practised for hours every day, training his feet to do what most people's hands cannot. His toes bled; his back ached from the strange position. Many times he wanted to give up, but he refused. Eventually, his playing became so skilful that he won a national talent competition, astonishing the whole country. Liu often says that in his life there were only ever two paths: to give up and die quickly, or to struggle and live well. 'I chose the second,' he says simply. His story reminds us that our limits are often far further away than we believe.

questions.prompt: What is Liu Wei known for?
options: Being a pianist who plays with his feet.

questions.prompt: How did Liu learn to play the piano?
options: He taught himself, because every teacher refused him.

questions.prompt: What does Liu's story mainly show?
options: That pianos are easy to play.
That our limits are often further than we believe.

questions.prompt: What were the 'two paths' Liu describes?
options: To play the piano or to sing.
To give up, or to struggle and live well.
To stay home or to travel.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 用脚弹琴的钢琴家)、细节(B 老师都拒收、他自学)、主旨(B 人的极限远比我们以为的更远)、细节(B 放弃速死 vs 抗争好好活)。

sets.passage: In a world that seems to move faster every year, a quiet movement is encouraging people to do the opposite: to slow down. Known as the 'Slow Movement', it began with food. Tired of fast food eaten in a hurry, some people argued that meals should be cooked carefully and enjoyed slowly, with family and friends. From food, the idea soon spread to other parts of life. Today there is talk of slow travel, slow reading, even slow cities. The central idea is simple but powerful: that in our rush to do more and more, we often lose the very things that make life worth living. We eat without tasting, travel without seeing, and meet without truly connecting. By slowing down, supporters say, we can pay more attention, enjoy experiences more deeply, and feel less stressed. Critics argue that slowing down is a luxury that busy, ordinary people cannot afford. Not everyone, they point out, has the time or money to spend hours cooking or to travel without a schedule. There is truth in this. Yet the heart of the Slow Movement is not really about doing everything slowly. It is about being thoughtful — about choosing where to spend our limited time and attention, rather than rushing mindlessly through our days. In that sense, its message is one that almost anyone, however busy, can use.

questions.prompt: Where did the Slow Movement begin?
What is the central idea of the movement?
options: That in our rush we often lose what makes life worth living.

questions.prompt: What do critics argue?
options: That slowing down is a luxury ordinary people cannot afford.
That cities are too slow already.

questions.prompt: What is the real heart of the movement?
options: Doing absolutely everything as slowly as possible.
Being thoughtful about where we spend our time and attention.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 始于饮食)、细节(B 匆忙中丢失了让生活有意义的东西)、细节(B 批评者称慢是奢侈)、主旨/推断(B 核心是有意识地选择时间与注意力的去处)。

sets.passage: When we think of the food we eat, we rarely think of bees. Yet these small insects are among the most important workers in the world's food supply. As bees move from flower to flower collecting nectar, they carry pollen with them, allowing plants to produce fruit and seeds. This process, called pollination, is essential for a huge number of the crops we depend on, from apples and almonds to coffee and cotton. In recent years, however, bee populations in many parts of the world have been falling alarmingly. Scientists believe several causes are at work. The widespread use of certain chemicals on farms can harm or kill bees. The loss of wild flowers, as land is turned into farms and cities, leaves bees with less food. Diseases and a changing climate add further pressure. The decline of bees is not only a problem for nature lovers; it is a threat to our food. Without enough bees, the cost of many foods would rise sharply, and some might become rare luxuries. The good news is that people can help. Planting flowers, reducing the use of harmful chemicals, and protecting wild areas all give bees a better chance. Some cities have even begun keeping bees on rooftops. Protecting these tiny creatures, it turns out, is really about protecting ourselves.

questions.prompt: Why are bees so important for our food?
options: They make honey and nothing else.
They pollinate the crops we depend on.
They clean the air for us.

questions.prompt: What is one cause of falling bee numbers?
options: Harmful chemicals and the loss of wild flowers.

questions.prompt: Why is the decline of bees a threat to us?
options: Without bees, many foods would become costly or rare.
Bees clean our homes for us.
It is actually not a threat.

questions.prompt: What is the passage mainly about?
options: The importance of bees and how we can protect them.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 为作物授粉)、细节(B 农药与野花消失)、推断(B 没蜜蜂食物会变贵变稀)、主旨(B 蜜蜂的重要性与保护之道)。

sets.passage: The rise of online learning has been one of the biggest changes in education in recent years. With a computer and an internet connection, a student in a small village can now take courses from the world's best universities, often for free. For many, this has opened doors that were once firmly closed. The advantages are clear. Online courses are flexible, allowing people to study at their own pace and to fit learning around work and family. They can also be far cheaper than traditional education, and they make a vast range of subjects available to anyone, anywhere. Yet online learning also has real weaknesses. Without a classroom, a teacher's eye, or classmates around them, many students find it hard to stay motivated, and a large number give up before finishing. Learning alone in front of a screen can feel lonely, and it cannot easily teach the social skills that come from working with others. There is also the problem of unequal access: those without good computers or internet — often the very people who would benefit most — are left behind. Most educators now believe that the future lies not in choosing between online and traditional learning, but in combining the best of both. Used wisely, online tools can make education more open and flexible; but they work best alongside, not instead of, real human contact.

questions.prompt: What is one advantage of online learning?
options: It is flexible and often cheaper, with many subjects available.
It needs no work at all.
It always has a teacher in the room.

questions.prompt: What is one weakness of online learning?
options: Many students find it hard to stay motivated and give up.
There are no subjects to study.

questions.prompt: What is the problem of unequal access?
options: Those without good computers or internet are left behind.
The courses cost too much money.

questions.prompt: What do most educators believe about the future?
options: Online learning will completely replace schools.
The future lies in combining online and traditional learning.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 灵活、更便宜、科目丰富)、细节(B 难以坚持、很多人放弃)、细节(B 无好设备者被落下)、主旨/推断(B 未来在于线上线下结合)。

sets.passage: Twenty years ago, the small town of Riverton was slowly dying. Its factories had closed, its young people had left for the cities, and its main street was lined with empty shops. Few believed the town had a future. Today, however, Riverton is full of life again, and its story offers hope to struggling towns everywhere. The change did not come from a big company or a government plan, but from the townspeople themselves. A group of residents, refusing to give up on their home, began with small steps. They cleaned up the river, planted gardens, and turned empty shops into art studios and small cafes. They organised a weekend market where local farmers and craftspeople could sell their goods, which slowly began to attract visitors from nearby cities. Success did not come overnight. For years, progress was slow, and many doubted it would work. But bit by bit, the town grew livelier. New small businesses opened, some young people returned, and a sense of pride came back to the community. Riverton's recovery was not the result of a single clever idea or a large sum of money. It grew, instead, from the patience and determination of ordinary people who loved their town and refused to let it die. Their story shows that the future of a community often lies, above all, in the hands of the people who live there.

questions.prompt: What was Riverton like twenty years ago?
options: Slowly dying, with closed factories and empty shops.

questions.prompt: Who brought about the change in Riverton?
What did the residents do first?
options: Cleaned the river and turned empty shops into studios and cafes.
Left the town for the cities.
Waited for the government to help.

questions.prompt: What does Riverton's story show?
options: Only money can save a town.
A community's future often lies in the hands of its own people.
Factories should never be allowed to close.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 衰落:工厂关、店铺空)、细节(B 由镇民自己推动)、细节(B 先清河、把空店改成工作室咖啡馆)、主旨(B 社区的未来在本地人手中)。
